summary: Large Kirby init/special-N decompilation slice: matched 5.97% of `melee/ft/chara/ftKirby/ftKb_Init.c` for a 127.51 KB file, adding many newly decompiled functions and the header/type scaffolding needed for Kirby hats, copied neutral-B variants, items, motion vars, and data attributes. The PR had no review comments, so conclusions are based on the diff and generated match report.
what_changed: Added 859 lines to `ftKb_Init.c`, including many newly decompiled Kirby init and copied-special functions reported as 100% matched, such as `ftKb_Init_OnItemPickup`, `ftKb_Init_UnkDemoCallbacks0`, `ftKb_SpecialN_800EFAF0`, `ftKb_SpecialN_800EFB4C`, `ftKb_SpecialN_800EFE80`, `ftKb_SpecialAirLw_800F5524`, `ftKb_SpecialN_800F5B3C`, `ftKb_SpecialN_800F5F68`, `fn_800F6178`, `fn_800F6AC8`, `ftKb_Eat_*`, `ftKb_SpecialNNs_*`, `ftKb_NsSpecialNStart_Anim`, `ftKb_NsSpecialNHold_Anim`, `ftKb_SpecialNMs_8010B2FC`, and several Peach copied-special helpers.

Expanded `src/melee/ft/chara/ftKirby/types.h` substantially: renamed `hat.victim_kind` to `hat.kind`, renamed `hat.x18` to `hat.unk_hsd_obj`, added `ns_flash_gobj`, `xD0`, `xE0`-style state fields, added `ftKb_MotionVars`, and replaced a mostly filler `ftKb_DatAttrs` with a detailed 0x434-byte attribute map grouped by Kirby move and copied character special.
Added `struct ftKb_SpecialNMs_DatAttrs` for Marth/Roy copied neutral-B data and embedded it as `ms, fe` inside `ftKb_DatAttrs`.
Added `Kirby_Unk` in `ft_0877.h` and changed `ft_80459B88` from `s32[FTKIND_MAX]` to `Kirby_Unk* [FTKIND_MAX]`, supporting typed access to Kirby hat joint/accessory data.
Changed `Fighter` core types to include `union ftKb_MotionVars kb` in motion vars, and changed `x1A64` from `s32` to `UNK_T`.
Added or refined item/library prototypes needed by the new C code, including `it_802F23EC`, `it_802F28C8`, Kirby hammer item functions, `lbMthp_8001E8F8`, `it_802AA7F0`, and `it_802AA8C0`.
Turned `ftParts_8007487C` back into an unresolved documented symbol in `ftparts.c` and loosened its prototype to `UNK_T` parameters, matching call sites that pass Kirby hat model data.

Added dox stub files for `ftKb_Init`, `ftcolanim`, `itkirby_2F23`, and `lbmthp`, and added documentation comments for several existing functions such as `ft_80084F3C` as `Fighter_PhysGround_ApplyFriction` and `ftData_80085E50` as `Animation_GetAddress`.

The asm file `asm/melee/ft/chara/ftKirby/ftKb_Init.s` was massively regenerated/updated, with labels and instruction addresses moving from zero-based scratch offsets to real addresses such as `800EE528` through `8010D740`, consistent with the matched C slice.
smart_moves: Recovered large attribute layout incrementally but safely with explicit byte offsets and `STATIC_ASSERT(sizeof(struct ftKb_DatAttrs) == 0x434)`, preventing silent drift while many fields remain only partially understood.

Replaced magic fighter-kind numbers in matched code with `FTKIND_*` constants, e.g. Donkey Kong, Samus, Mewtwo, and Sheik cases in `ftKb_Init_UnkMotionStates4`, improving readability without changing behavior.
Created small nested structs/unions for Kirby-specific runtime state (`ftKb_FighterVars`, `ftKb_MotionVars`, `ftKb_SpecialNMs_DatAttrs`) rather than spreading raw offsets throughout the new matches.

Used documentation and TODO comments to mark weakly understood areas instead of overclaiming, including `Bad cast`, `Named flags`, `Every hat is its own struct`, and comments about unknown hat/model data.
Added precise dependency prototypes in nearby subsystem headers instead of leaving call sites as untyped placeholders, which helps future matching work in Kirby, item, and animation code.
Preserved matching-sensitive ordering and scoping patterns in `ftKb_Init.c`, including nested blocks, local redeclarations, `PAD_STACK`, and static flag constants where needed for codegen.
decomp_lessons: For large fighter init files, matching many small 100% functions often requires broad header/type cleanup first: fighter vars, item prototypes, baselib object functions, animation helpers, and data attributes all interact.
Kirby copied-special code benefits from grouping `ftKb_DatAttrs` by source character and by move; this makes offsets usable while preserving exact layout through static asserts.

When a global's real type is uncovered, update the global declaration rather than casting everywhere. This PR changed `ft_80459B88` to a typed `Kirby_Unk*` table, leaving only a documented bad cast where the old scratch pattern still required it.
Use enum types and `FTKIND_*` constants as soon as possible in switch statements; they communicate decompiled intent and reduce risk when comparing copied ability behavior across characters.

Do not force premature precision into unknown function signatures. `ftParts_8007487C` was relaxed to `UNK_T` arguments because Kirby hat setup passes several model/parts objects whose final types were not yet proven.
Adding dox stubs for functions used by a match can be a low-risk way to document symbol purpose and unblock compilation without implying full implementation.

Callbacks are central to Kirby copied-special cleanup: newly matched code assigns death, damage, hitlag, accessory, and state-change callbacks, so future work should track callback lifetimes alongside item/object ownership.
naming_conventions: `ftKb_` prefix is used for Kirby fighter functions; copied neutral-B variants include source-character fragments such as `SpecialNNs`, `SpecialNPe`, `SpecialNMs`, `SpecialNGw`, and `SpecialNYs`.
Unknown static helpers retain address-based names like `fn_800F6178`, `fn_8010C344`, `fn_8010C44C`, and `fn_8010CD88` when no behavior-backed name is proven.

Kirby fighter-vars fields retain offset-style names (`xD0`, `xE0`, `xF4_b0`) where meaning is unknown, but obvious fields were renamed to descriptive names such as `hat.kind`, `hat.unk_hsd_obj`, and `ns_flash_gobj`.

Kirby data-attribute names use `specialn_<character-abbrev>_<purpose>` for copied neutral-B data, e.g. `specialn_ss_charge_time`, `specialn_dk_swings_to_full_charge`, `specialn_mt_charge_time`, `specialn_ns_charge_release_delay`, and `specialn_pe_absorbdesc`.
Headers use project address comments for external symbols, e.g. `/* 2F23EC */ void it_802F23EC(...)` and `/* 01E8F8 */ float lbMthp_8001E8F8(u32);`.
New include guards for item headers use GALE01 address-style names, e.g. `GALE01_2ADC04` and `GALE01_2F23AC`.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: The PR match report shows many small functions at 100% with score 0, while a few larger functions remained near-matches, indicating a strategy of landing clean leaf/helper functions first inside a very large file.
`PAD_STACK` was used in multiple newly decompiled functions to preserve stack layout where ordinary C locals would not match.

Nested scopes and local redeclarations were retained in functions such as `ftKb_SpecialNNs_800FED38`, `ftKb_SpecialNNs_800FEDD0`, and `ftKb_SpecialNMs_8010B2FC`, likely to force register allocation and lifetime behavior.
A static local motion-state flag mask in `fn_800F6AC8` was written as bit shifts with a `@todo Named flags` comment, preserving exact constants while documenting a future cleanup path.
Real-address labels in the regenerated asm (`.L_800EE540`, `.L_800EE5B0`, etc.) replaced zero-based scratch labels, making the remaining assembly easier to correlate with map addresses.
Typed function prototypes were adjusted to match call-site codegen, such as `ftData_80085E50(Fighter*, enum_t msid)`, `it_802AA8C0(..., enum_t, float)`, and `lbMthp_8001E8F8` returning `float`.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or reviews were present in the provided PR context.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that rename `fn_800F6178`, `fn_8010C344`, `fn_8010C44C`, or `fn_8010CD88`.
Check later Kirby PRs for further splitting of `ftKb_DatAttrs` into per-hat structs, as noted by the TODO.
Search for cleanup of `ftParts_8007487C` argument types after Kirby hat model data is better understood.
Look for later replacement of magic motion IDs such as `436` and color animation IDs such as `58`, `54`, `93`, `87`, `99`, and `100` with enums.
Trace later handling of `ft_80459B88` and `Kirby_Unk` to see whether `x0`, `x4`, `x8`, `xC`, and `x10` received semantic names.
